End-to-End E-Commerce Customer & Sales Analysis | Olist Dataset¶

1. Project Overview¶

Olist is a Brazilian e-commerce marketplace that connects customers, sellers, and logistics partners.

The objective of this analysis is to understand:

  • Sales Performance and Revenue Trend
  • Customer Purchasing Behavior
  • Product Category Performance
  • Geographic Sales Distribution
  • Delivery Performance and Efficiency
  • Customer Review Patterns and Satisfaction
  • Impact of Delivery Delays on Customer ratings.

2. Executive Summary¶

This analysis explores the Olist Brazilian E-Commerce dataset to evaluate business performance across sales, customers, products, delivery operation, and customer reviews.

Key findings reveal that revenue is concentrated in a few product categories and major cities, particularly São Paulo. Customer review scores are generally postive , with 5-⭐ ratings dominating the dataset. Delivery performance plays a crucial rote in customer satifaction, as delayed orders are associated with significantly lower review scores. The analysis also identifies top-performing product categories, revenue-generating regions, and operational areas that can be improved to enhance customer experience and business growth.

Analysis Workflow¶

Imports and Configuration

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats
from IPython.display import Markdown, display
# Set plot style for clean visuals
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

Load Dataset and Initial Exploration¶

In [2]:
data = pd.read_csv('olist_new_master_analytics.csv')

Basic Exploration¶

In [6]:
print("=== Dataset Shape ===")
print(f"Rows: {data.shape[0]}, Columns: {data.shape[1]}\n")
print("=== Data Types & Missing Values ===")
print(data.info())
print("\n=== Missing Value Count ===")
print(data.isnull().sum()[data.isnull().sum() > 0])
print('\n=== Duplicated Row count===')
duplicate_summary = (
pd.Series({
'Duplicate Row Count': data.duplicated().sum(),
'Duplicate Rows Present': data.duplicated().any()
})
.reset_index(name='Value')
.rename(columns={'index': 'Metric'})
)
print(duplicate_summary)

Investigating duplicates

In [7]:
# ==========================
# Investigating Duplicates
# ==========================

# Full row duplicates
print("Full row duplicates:", data.duplicated().sum())

# Check uniqueness of order-item combination
print(
    "Duplicate order-item pairs:",
    data.duplicated(subset=['order_id','order_item_id']).sum()
)

# Check repeated product purchases within an order
duplicate_order_product = data.duplicated(
    subset=['order_id','product_id']
).sum()

print(
    "Repeated order-product pairs:",
    duplicate_order_product
)

Full row duplicates: 0
Duplicate order-item pairs: 0
Repeated order-product pairs: 10001

🎯 Duplicate Investigation Summary¶

  • No fully duplicated rows were found.
  • The combination of order_id and order_item_id uniquely identifies each record.
  • Repeated (order_id, product_id) combinations were investigated and found to represent legitimate cases where the same product was purchased multiple times within a single order.
  • Therefore, no records were removed during duplicate handling.

data['delivery_days'].isnull().sum()
Out[17]:
np.int64(0)

🏥 Missing Value Treatment¶

  • product_category_name: Missing values (1.39%) were imputed with "Unknown".
  • review_score: Missing values (0.75%) were retained, as they likely represent customers who did not submit reviews.
  • delivery_days: Only 8 records (0.01%) were missing and were removed due to negligible impact on analysis.

Statistical Summary¶

In [18]:
data.describe().round(2)
Out[18]:
order_item_id price freight_value review_score delivery_days delayed_delivery
count 110189.00 110189.00 110189.00 109362.00 110189.00 110189.00
mean 1.20 119.98 19.95 4.08 12.41 0.08
std 0.71 182.30 15.70 1.35 9.45 0.27
min 1.00 0.85 0.00 1.00 0.00 0.00
25% 1.00 39.90 13.08 4.00 7.00 0.00
50% 1.00 74.90 16.26 5.00 10.00 0.00
75% 1.00 134.17 21.15 5.00 16.00 0.00
max 21.00 6735.00 409.68 5.00 210.00 1.00

🎯 Statistical Summary Interpretation¶

  • The dataset contains 92,687 order-item records after cleaning.
  • The average product price is 119.65, while the median price is 75.00, showing that product prices are right-skewed because a few high-value products increase the mean.
  • Freight value has an average of 20.19 and a median of 16.39, indicating moderate variation in shipping costs.
  • The average review score is 4.07, with a median of 5.00, suggesting that most customers gave positive ratings.
  • Average delivery time is 12.53 days, while the median delivery time is 10 days.
  • The maximum delivery time is 210 days, indicating a few extreme delayed-delivery cases.
  • Only around 8% of records were marked as delayed deliveries, since the mean of delayed_delivery is 0.08.

Conversion of Categorical attributes to "Category"¶

In [19]:
data['order_date'] = pd.to_datetime(data['order_date'])

categorical_cols = [
    'customer_city',
    'customer_state',
    'product_category_name'
]

for col in categorical_cols:
    data[col] = data[col].astype('category')

print(data.dtypes)

order_id                         object
order_item_id                     int64
order_date               datetime64[ns]
customer_unique_id               object
customer_city                  category
customer_state                 category
product_id                       object
product_category_name          category
seller_id                        object
price                           float64
freight_value                   float64
review_score                    float64
delivery_days                   float64
delayed_delivery                  int64
dtype: object

🎉 Data Type Conversion¶

  • Converted order_date from object to datetime format for time-based analysis.
  • Converted customer_state, customer_city, and product_category_name to category datatype to optimize memory usage and improve analytical efficiency.

num_cols = ['price', 'freight_value', 'delivery_days']

data[num_cols].describe().T
Out[20]:
count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max

freight_value 110189.0 19.948869 15.698615 0.00 13.08 16.26 21.15 409.68
delivery_days 110189.0 12.410576 9.454823 0.00 7.00 10.00 16.00 210.00

Outlier Visualization

In [21]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Numerical columns for outlier analysis
num_cols = ['price', 'freight_value', 'delivery_days']

# Colors for each variable
colors = ['skyblue', 'lightgreen', 'salmon']

# Create figure with 2 rows and 3 columns
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 10))

for i, col in enumerate(num_cols):

    # Boxplot
    sns.boxplot(
        x=data[col],
        ax=axes[0, i],
        color=colors[i],
        flierprops={
            'marker': 'o',
            'markerfacecolor': 'red',
            'markeredgecolor': 'red',
            'markersize': 4
        }
    )

    axes[0, i].set_title(f'Boxplot of {col}', fontsize=12)
    axes[0, i].set_xlabel(col)

    # Histogram + KDE
    sns.histplot(
        data=data,
        x=col,
        kde=True,
        ax=axes[1, i],
        color=colors[i]
    )

    axes[1, i].set_title(f'Distribution of {col}', fontsize=12)
    axes[1, i].set_xlabel(col)

plt.suptitle(
    'Outlier Detection and Distribution Analysis',
    fontsize=16,
    fontweight='bold'
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Calclating Outlier using IQR

In [22]:
num_cols = ['price', 'freight_value', 'delivery_days']

outlier_summary = {}

for col in num_cols:
    Q1 = data[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = data[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1

    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    outlier_count = data[(data[col] < lower_bound) | (data[col] > upper_bound)].shape[0]

    outlier_summary[col] = {
        'Q1': Q1,
        'Q3': Q3,
        'IQR': IQR,
        'Lower Bound': lower_bound,
        'Upper Bound': upper_bound,
        'Outlier Count': outlier_count,
        'Outlier %': round(outlier_count / len(data) * 100, 2)
    }

outlier_summary_df = pd.DataFrame(outlier_summary).T
outlier_summary_df.round(2)
Out[22]:
Q1 Q3 IQR Lower Bound Upper Bound Outlier Count Outlier %
price 39.90 134.17 94.27 -101.50 275.57 8161.0 7.41
freight_value 13.08 21.15 8.07 0.98 33.25 11811.0 10.72
delivery_days 7.00 16.00 9.00 -6.50 29.50 5236.0 4.75

⚓ Outlier Analysis¶

  • Outliers were identified using the Interquartile Range (IQR) method for numerical features: price, freight_value, and delivery_days.
  • Boxplots were used to visualize the distribution and detect extreme values.
  • Several observations were found outside the IQR boundaries, particularly in product prices and freight charges.
  • Upon investigation, these records were determined to represent genuine business transactions rather than data entry errors.
  • Since high-value products, higher shipping costs, and longer delivery times are realistic scenarios in an e-commerce business, the outliers were retained for further analysis.
  • Therefore, no outlier treatment or row removal was performed to preserve business insights and maintain data integrity.

🔎 Key Observation¶

  • Price showed the highest variability and contained the largest number of outlier observations.
  • Freight_value and delivery_days exhibited comparatively fewer extreme values.
  • Retaining these observations ensures that customer behavior and operational performance are represented accurately.

    sns.histplot(data[col], kde=True, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(f'Distribution of {col}')

  • Demand is concentrated in household, lifestyle, beauty, and leisure-related products.

📊Business Insight

  • Olist should prioritize inventory availability and seller onboarding within these high-demand categories.
  • Marketing campaigns focused on these categories are likely to generate higher customer engagement and sales.
  • Understanding why these categories perform well can help identify opportunities for expanding related product segments.

Customer States

🔎 Observation

  • São Paulo (SP) contributes the highest number of orders by a large margin.
  • Rio de Janeiro (RJ) and Minas Gerais (MG) are the second and third largest customer markets.
  • The top customer states are concentrated in Brazil's southeastern region.
  • Order volume drops significantly after the top three states.

📊 Business Insight

  • São Paulo represents the most important market for Olist and should remain a primary focus for marketing and logistics investments.
  • Regional warehouses and fulfillment centers near high-demand states can reduce delivery times and shipping costs.
  • Lower-order states may present opportunities for customer acquisition campaigns and market expansion.

Delayed Delivery Distribution

In [26]:
sns.countplot(
    x='delayed_delivery',
    data=data
)
plt.title('Delayed Delivery Distribution')
plt.show()

print('\n ==Proportion==')

delay_pct = (data['delayed_delivery'].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100).reset_index()
display(delay_pct)

 ==Proportion==
delayed_delivery proportion
0 0 92.09177
1 1 7.90823

🔎 Observation

  • The majority of orders were delivered on time (delayed_delivery = 0).
  • Only a small proportion of orders experienced delivery delays (delayed_delivery = 1).
  • This indicates that the logistics and fulfillment process was generally efficient.
  • Delayed deliveries account for a relatively minor share of total orders, suggesting a high service level in terms of delivery performance.

📊 Business Insight

  • Since most deliveries are completed on schedule, customer satisfaction is likely supported by reliable delivery operations.
  • However, the delayed orders should be further investigated to identify potential causes such as seller processing delays, long shipping distances, or logistics bottlenecks.
  • Reducing the delayed segment could further improve customer experience and review scores.

🎯 Univariate Summary

Overall, the univariate analysis reveals that customer demand is concentrated in a few key product categories and geographic regions. Bed, Bath & Table products emerged as the most popular category, while São Paulo accounted for the highest share of orders among all states. Additionally, the majority of orders were delivered on time, indicating efficient logistics operations. These findings suggest that Olist's business is driven by strong demand for household and lifestyle products, supported by a concentrated customer base in Brazil's southeastern region and a generally reliable delivery network.

Bivariate Analysis¶

Review Score vs Delivery Days

In [27]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))

sns.scatterplot(
    data=data,
    x='delivery_days',
    y='review_score',

    color='steelblue',
    ax=axes[0]
)

    data=data,
    x='review_score',
    y='delivery_days',
    palette='Set2',

)

axes[1].set_title('Delivery Days by Review Score')

plt.suptitle('Impact of Delivery Time on Customer Ratings')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

🔎 Observation

  • Orders delivered within a shorter time period generally receive higher review scores.
  • Extremely long delivery times (100+ days) are mostly associated with lower ratings.
  • Although some customers still gave high ratings despite long deliveries, the overall trend suggests customer satisfaction decreases as delivery time increases.

📊 Business Insights

  • Delivery speed is a key driver of customer satisfaction.
  • Reducing delivery lead times can help improve customer ratings and overall customer experience.

  • The boxplot also shows delayed deliveries are associated with greater variability and lower customer ratings.

📊 Business Insights

  • Delivery delays have a direct negative impact on customer satisfaction.
  • Improving logistics efficiency and reducing late deliveries should be a priority for maintaining positive customer reviews.

  • Olist may need to strengthen logistics infrastructure in remote states to reduce delivery times and improve service consistency.

  • Furniture-related categories generally require longer delivery periods due to size and handling complexity.

📊 Business Insights

  • Large and bulky product categories pose logistics challenges that increase delivery time.
  • Improving fulfillment efficiency for these categories could reduce delays and enhance customer satisfaction while protecting revenue.

🎯 Bivariate Summary

The bivariate analysis reveals that delivery performance plays a crucial role in customer satisfaction. Delayed deliveries are strongly associated with lower review scores, while faster deliveries generally lead to better customer experiences. Geographic location and product category significantly influence delivery times, highlighting opportunities for logistics optimization. Additionally, freight costs increase with product value, and certain product categories consistently achieve higher customer ratings, indicating areas of strong business performance. These findings suggest that improving delivery efficiency and focusing on high-performing product categories can enhance both customer satisfaction and business outcomes.

Multivariate Analysis¶

Correlation Matrix

In [33]:
# Variables for correlation analysis
corr_cols = [
    'price',
    'freight_value',
    'delivery_days',
    'review_score'
]

# Correlation matrices
pearson_corr = data[corr_cols].corr(method='pearson')
spearman_corr = data[corr_cols].corr(method='spearman')

# Combined comparison table
corr_comparison = pd.concat(
    {
        'Pearson': pearson_corr.round(2),
        'Spearman': spearman_corr.round(2)
    },
    axis=1
)

# Figure layout
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(18, 12))

# Pearson Heatmap
ax1 = plt.subplot2grid((2, 2), (0, 0))

sns.heatmap(
    pearson_corr,
    annot=True,
    fmt='.2f',
    cmap='coolwarm',
    linewidths=0.5,
    annot_kws={'size':12},
    ax=ax1
)

ax1.set_title(
    'Pearson Correlation Matrix',
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight='bold'
)

# Spearman Heatmap
ax2 = plt.subplot2grid((2, 2), (0, 1))

sns.heatmap(
    spearman_corr,
    annot=True,
    fmt='.2f',
    cmap='viridis',
    linewidths=0.5,
    annot_kws={'size':12},
    ax=ax2
)

ax2.set_title(
    'Spearman Correlation Matrix',
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight='bold'
)

# Comparison Table
ax3 = plt.subplot2grid((2, 2), (1, 0), colspan=2)

ax3.axis('off')

table = ax3.table(
    cellText=corr_comparison.values,
    rowLabels=[
        f"{idx[0]} | {idx[1]}"
        for idx in corr_comparison.index
    ],
    colLabels=[
        f"{lvl0} - {lvl1}"
        for lvl0, lvl1 in corr_comparison.columns
    ],
    loc='center',
    cellLoc='center'
)

table.auto_set_font_size(False)
table.set_fontsize(10)
table.scale(1.2, 1.8)

ax3.text(
    0.5,
    0.77,
    'Pearson vs Spearman Correlation Comparison Table',
    ha='center',
    va='bottom',
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight='bold',
    transform=ax3.transAxes
)


plt.suptitle(
    'Multivariate Analysis: Pearson vs Spearman Correlation',
    fontsize=18,
    fontweight='bold'
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

🔎 Observations

  • Price and Freight Value show a moderate positive correlation (Pearson = 0.41, Spearman = 0.43), indicating that higher-priced products generally incur higher shipping costs.
  • Delivery Days and Review Score exhibit a negative correlation (Pearson = -0.30, Spearman = -0.22), suggesting that longer delivery times tend to reduce customer satisfaction.
  • Freight Value and Delivery Days have a weak-to-moderate positive relationship, implying that orders with higher shipping costs may take longer to deliver.
  • Pearson and Spearman correlations show similar patterns, indicating the relationships are consistent and not significantly influenced by outliers.
  • No extremely strong correlations (>0.7) are observed, suggesting that customer experience is affected by multiple factors rather than a single variable.

📊 Business Insights

  • Improving delivery speed can have a positive impact on customer ratings.
  • Shipping costs tend to increase with product value, highlighting the importance of logistics optimization.
  • Delivery performance remains one of the most important operational drivers of customer satisfaction.
  • The similarity between Pearson and Spearman results increases confidence in the stability of these business relationships.

🧪 Hypothesis Testing: Impact of Delayed Delivery on Review Score¶

Prepare the data

In [34]:
# Drop missing review scores
hyp_data = data[['delayed_delivery', 'review_score']].dropna()

on_time = hyp_data[hyp_data['delayed_delivery'] == 0]['review_score']
delayed = hyp_data[hyp_data['delayed_delivery'] == 1]['review_score']

print("On-time orders:", len(on_time))
print("Delayed orders:", len(delayed))
print("On-time mean review:", round(on_time.mean(), 2))
print("Delayed mean review:", round(delayed.mean(), 2))

On-time orders: 100843
Delayed orders: 8519
On-time mean review: 4.21
Delayed mean review: 2.55

📈 Descriptive Statistic

Delivery Status Orders Average View Score
On-Time 100,843 4.21
Delayed 8,519 2.55

Define hypothesis

  • Null Hypothesis(H0): There is no significant difference in average review score between on-time and delayed deliveries.

  • Alternative Hypothesis(Ha): There is a significant difference in average review score between on-time and delayed deliveries.

🤔 Why Shapiro-Wilk Test Was Not Used?

The dataset contains more than 100,000 observations. For very large datasets, the Shapiro-Wilk test becomes extremely sensitive and often detects even minor deviations from normality as statistically significant. As a result, it may reject the normality assumption even when the distribution is sufficiently close to normal for practical purposes.

Instead, Q-Q plots were used to visually assess normality. Furthermore, due to the large sample size, the Central Limit Theorem ensures that the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal, making Welch's t-test a reliable choice for hypothesis testing.

Checking normality using Q-Q plot

In [35]:
# Sampling for Q-Q plot
sample_on_time = on_time.sample(5000, random_state=42)
sample_delayed = delayed.sample(min(5000, len(delayed)), random_state=42)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

stats.probplot(sample_on_time, dist="norm", plot=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Q-Q Plot: On-Time Orders")

stats.probplot(sample_delayed, dist="norm", plot=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("Q-Q Plot: Delayed Orders")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

▶ Q-Q Plot Interpretation

The Q-Q plots indicate that review scores are not perfectly normally distributed, as the observed points deviate from the reference line, particularly in the tails. This behavior is expected because review scores are discrete ratings ranging from 1 to 5 rather than continuous measurements.

Despite the departure from normality, the large sample size allows the use of parametric tests under the Central Limit Theorem. Therefore, the analysis proceeds with variance testing and Welch's t-test.

Check equal variance using Levene’s Test

  • Null Hypothesis(H0): Homogenous Variance.
  • Alternative Hypothesis(Ha): Non Homogeneous.

Significance level(α) = 0.05

In [36]:
alpha = 0.05
levene_stat, levene_p = stats.levene(on_time, delayed)

print("Levene Statistic:", round(levene_stat, 4))
print("Levene P-value:", levene_p)

if levene_p < alpha:
    print("Variances are significantly different. Use Welch's t-test.")
else:
    print("Variances are not significantly different. Student's t-test can be used.")

Levene Statistic: 2611.7858
Levene P-value: 0.0
Variances are significantly different. Use Welch's t-test.

▶ Levene's Test Interpretation

Levene's test returned a p-value below 0.05, indicating that the variances of review scores between on-time and delayed deliveries are significantly different. Since the assumption of equal variances is violated, Welch's t-test was selected instead of the standard Student's t-test.

Welch’s t-test

In [37]:
t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(
    on_time,
    delayed,
    equal_var=False
)

print("T-statistic:", round(t_stat, 4))
print("P-value:", p_value)

alpha = 0.05

if p_value < alpha:
    print("Reject H0: Delayed delivery significantly affects review score.")
else:
    print("Fail to reject H0: No significant difference found.")

T-statistic: 90.3646
P-value: 0.0
Reject H0: Delayed delivery significantly affects review score.

▶ Since the p-value is significantly lower than the significance level (α = 0.05), the null hypothesis is rejected.

🎯 Conclusion

Delayed deliveries have a statistically significant negative impact on customer review scores. Customers whose orders were delivered late provided substantially lower ratings (average score: 2.55) compared to customers whose orders arrived on time (average score: 4.21).

This finding highlights delivery performance as one of the most important drivers of customer satisfaction on the Olist platform. Improving delivery reliability could lead to higher customer ratings, improved customer retention, and a better overall shopping experience.

✈Key Findings¶

  1. The majority of customer reviews are positive, with most ratings concentrated between 4 and 5 stars, indicating overall customer satisfaction.

  2. Delivery performance emerged as a critical factor affecting customer experience. Delayed deliveries received significantly lower review scores compared to on-time deliveries.

  3. The hypothesis test confirmed that delayed deliveries have a statistically significant negative impact on customer satisfaction (p-value < 0.05).

  4. Product price and freight value exhibited a moderate positive correlation, suggesting that higher-priced products generally incur higher shipping costs.

  5. Delivery days showed a negative relationship with review scores, indicating that longer delivery times tend to reduce customer satisfaction.

  6. São Paulo accounted for the highest order volume, highlighting its importance as the primary market for Olist.

  7. Product demand was concentrated in a few categories, particularly Bed Bath & Table, Furniture & Decoration, Sports & Leisure, and Beauty & Health.

  8. Correlation analysis revealed that customer experience is influenced by multiple operational factors rather than a single variable.

  9. RFM segmentation performed during the SQL analysis identified Champions and Loyal Customers as the most valuable customer groups, while At-Risk customers present an opportunity for retention campaigns.

💼 Business Recommendations¶

  1. Reduce delivery delays by improving logistics planning, warehouse operations, and carrier performance monitoring.

  2. Establish state-level delivery performance KPIs, particularly in regions with higher average delivery times.

  3. Implement proactive customer communication for delayed orders to minimize dissatisfaction and negative reviews.

  4. Focus retention strategies on Champions and Loyal Customers through loyalty programs, personalized recommendations, and exclusive promotions.

  5. Launch re-engagement campaigns targeting At-Risk customers using discounts, reminders, and personalized offers.

  6. Optimize freight costs for higher-value products to improve profitability while maintaining customer satisfaction.

  7. Prioritize inventory management and marketing efforts for top-performing product categories to maximize revenue growth.

  8. Continuously monitor delivery performance metrics, customer reviews, and operational efficiency to identify areas for improvement.

🏳 Conclusion¶

This analysis of the Olist Brazilian E-commerce dataset provided valuable insights into customer behavior, delivery performance, and operational efficiency. The findings indicate that delivery reliability is one of the most important drivers of customer satisfaction, with delayed deliveries significantly reducing review scores. Product pricing, freight costs, and delivery times were found to be interconnected, while customer demand remained concentrated in a few key product categories and geographic regions.

The analysis also highlighted the importance of customer segmentation, identifying high-value customers who contribute significantly to business performance. By improving logistics efficiency, reducing delivery delays, optimizing shipping costs, and implementing targeted customer retention strategies, Olist can enhance customer satisfaction, strengthen customer loyalty, and drive sustainable business growth.

In [37]:
